    cfg.source_formats = {"treat_as_html": [".html", ".htm"]}
elif args.format == "markdown":
    cfg.source_formats = {"treat_as_markdown": [".md"]}

That way, you can toggle the mode without editing YAML.

docs.title: How to Run the DITA ETL Pipeline on a Folder of Markdown Files
docs.text: This guide shows you how to run the pipeline against a directory of .md files, normalize them with Pandoc, and produce DITA 1.3 topics and a DITA map.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• macOS
  • Python 3.10+
  • Pandoc installed: brew install pandoc
  • Prefect installed via requirements.txt
  • Recommended: a virtual environment

docs.title: 1) Set up the project
docs.text:
cd /path/to/ETL-POC
python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
python -m pip install -U pip
python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
python -m pip install -e .

If packaging errors mention "Multiple top-level packages", ensure pyproject.toml installs only dita_etl.

docs.title: 2) Prefect orchestration options
docs.text:
Option A (recommended): run a dedicated Prefect server in another terminal

prefect server start
# then, in the terminal where you run the pipeline:
export PREFECT_API_URL="http://127.0.0.1:4200/api"

Option B: pin Prefect 2.x for simpler local execution

python -m pip install "prefect==2.16.9"

docs.title: 3) Configure inputs (optional)
docs.text:
Open config/config.yaml and confirm .md is treated as Markdown:

source_formats:
  treat_as_markdown: [".md"]

You can adjust classification heuristics in config/assess.yaml.

docs.title: 4) Run the pipeline on a directory of Markdown
docs.text: python scripts/cli.py --config config/config.yaml --input /absolute/path/to/markdown_dir

docs.title: What you'll get
docs.text:
• Stage 0 (Assessment) build/assess/
  • inventory.json per-file metrics and predictions
  • dedupe_map.json near-duplicate clusters
  • plans/*.conversion_plan.json per-file topicization plans

  • report.html a human-readable summary

  • DITA Output (Stages 1 3) build/out/

  • *.dita DITA topics (concept/task/reference)
  • out.ditamap DITA map referencing all topics

docs.title: 5) Troubleshooting
docs.text:
• ModuleNotFoundError: dita_etl use the same interpreter you installed into: python -m pip install -e . then python scripts/cli.py ...
  • Prefect ephemeral server timeout either run prefect server start and set PREFECT_API_URL, or install prefect==2.16.9.
  • pandoc: command not found install via Homebrew: brew install pandoc.
  • No topics produced verify the --input path; inspect build/assess/report.html to confirm files were seen.

docs.title: 6) Notes
docs.text:
• Markdown normalization: .md is read via Pandoc's GFM reader (-f gfm) for consistent headings/lists.
  • Topic boundaries: # becomes a new topic; ##+ become sections. Adjust in plans/*.conversion_plan.json if needed.
  • Classification: Heuristics route sections to concept/task/reference; tune in config/assess.yaml.
  • Media: Keep images next to source Markdown; relative links like ![alt](images/foo.png) are preserved.

docs.title: Markdown-first usage
docs.text:
The pipeline ingests Markdown out of the box. We normalize with Pandoc's GitHub-Flavored Markdown reader (-f gfm) before converting to DocBook and then DITA.

brew install pandoc
python3 -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
python -m pip install -U pip
python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
python -m pip install -e .
python scripts/cli.py --config config/config.yaml --input /path/to/markdown_dir

Notes: - Relative image/asset links in Markdown are preserved through normalization. - Heading policy: # starts a new topic; ##+ become sections. Heuristics pick concept/task/reference.

docs.title: Sanity checklist
docs.text:
• Ensure pandoc is on your PATH (pandoc -v).
  • If you use VS Code, select the venv interpreter ( P Python: Select Interpreter).
  • Prefer python from the venv (not the system python3) when running.

docs.title: How To: Configure and Read the ETL Assessment
docs.text:
This guide explains how to configure and interpret the Assessment Stage (Stage 0) in the ETL pipeline. The goal of this stage is to help you understand the structure, quality, and duplication risks in your source documents before they are converted into DITA XML.

The assessment is designed for support staff and engineers working with Component Content Management Systems (CCMS) who need to improve the reliability and semantic value of their content migration.

docs.title: 1. What the Assessment Does
docs.text:
The Assessment Stage scans your source documents (Markdown, HTML, DOCX, etc.) and produces:

  • inventory.json - machine-readable metrics for every file.
  • dedupe_map.json - clusters of duplicate and near-duplicate documents.
  • plans/*.conversion_plan.json - per-file guidance for how to chunk content and classify topics.
  • report.html - a human-readable dashboard that summarizes risk and readiness.

This gives you an evidence-based preview of conversion complexity and identifies documents that require cleanup or deduplication before automated conversion to DITA.

docs.title: 2. Configuring the Assessment
docs.text:
The assessment uses a YAML configuration file: config/assess.yaml.

Key sections you can adjust:

docs.title: Shingling and Duplicate Detection
docs.text:
shingling:
  ngram: 7                 # how many tokens per shingle
  minhash_num_perm: 64     # number of hash permutations
  threshold: 0.88          # similarity threshold (0-1) for near duplicates
- Lower the threshold to detect looser matches (more aggressive dedupe). - Increase ngram for stricter text chunking.

docs.title: Scoring Weights
docs.text:
scoring:
  topicization_weights:
    heading_ladder_valid: 10
    avg_section_len_target: 15
    tables_simple: 10
    lists_depth_ok: 10
    images_with_alt: 5
  risk_weights:
    deep_nesting: 20
    complex_tables: 25
    unresolved_anchors: 15
    mixed_inline_blocks: 10
- Adjust weights to reflect what matters most in your content (e.g., penalize complex tables more heavily if your downstream XSLT struggles with them).

docs.title: Classification Rules
docs.text:
classification:
  task_keywords: ["click","run","open","select","type","press"]
  task_landmarks: ["prerequisites","steps","results","troubleshooting"]
  reference_markers: ["parameters","options","syntax","defaults"]
- Tune keywords so the system better predicts whether a section is a concept, task, or reference topic.

docs.title: Limits
docs.text:
limits:
  target_section_tokens: [50, 500]
- Defines the "ideal" section size for DITA topics. Shorter or longer sections reduce the readiness score.

docs.title: 3. Running the Assessment
docs.text:
Run the pipeline normally:

python scripts/cli.py --config config/config.yaml --input /path/to/source

The assessment will run as Stage 0, producing artifacts under:

build/assess/
      inventory.json
      dedupe_map.json
      report.html
      plans/
          file1.md.conversion_plan.json
          file2.docx.conversion_plan.json
      ...

docs.title: Report (report.html)
docs.text:
Open build/assess/report.html in a browser. It shows:

  • File list with size, section count, readiness score, and risk score.
  • Duplicate clusters listing files that are exact or near-duplicates.

Use this as your first stop for a quick overview.

docs.title: Inventory (inventory.json)
docs.text:
This JSON contains detailed metrics per file: - size (bytes), sections (count), sha256 (fingerprint). - topicization_readiness (0-100): higher means easier to split into DITA topics. - conversion_risk (0-100): higher means more structural problems (deep nesting, complex tables, etc.). - predictions: per-section suggested type (concept, task, reference).

This is useful for automation or dashboards.

docs.title: Duplicate Map (dedupe_map.json)
docs.text: Lists clusters of files that are highly similar. Each cluster is an array of file paths. Use this to: - Remove redundant files before conversion. - Decide which copy is canonical.

docs.title: Conversion Plans (plans/*.conversion_plan.json)
docs.text:
Each file gets a plan with: - chunking: rules for splitting into topics (e.g., split at H1 headings). - default_topic_type: predicted type if no per-section classification is applied. - sections: predictions for each section, with confidence scores and reasons.

You can feed these into the transformation stage to influence DITA output.

docs.title: 5. Using Assessment Results to Improve ETL
docs.text:
1. Deduplication - Quarantine duplicates before feeding into Extract/Transform. This avoids wasted effort and redundant topics in DITA.
  2. Chunking Strategy - Adjust conversion rules based on readiness scores. Poor scores often mean inconsistent heading usage; fix these in the source.
  3. Classification Tuning - Update assess.yaml classification rules when predictions don't match your authoring model.
  4. Prioritization - High-risk files should be reviewed manually before conversion; low-risk files can be converted automatically.

docs.title: 6. Best Practices
docs.text:
• Iterate configs: Adjust weights and rules in assess.yaml as you learn what patterns your corpus has.
  • Version configs: Keep assess.yaml in version control alongside the content model so assessments are reproducible.
  • Review report.html regularly: It's your early-warning system for bad inputs.
  • Automate quarantine: Integrate duplicate clusters and high-risk flags into your pipeline to automatically skip or isolate problematic files.

docs.title: How To: Change the Extractor for “Specify source content”
docs.text:
This guide explains how to switch, modify, or replace the extractor used to pull source content into the pipeline’s intermediate DocBook (the “Specify source content” step). It covers:

  • How the extractor registry works
  • Switching extractors by config only (no code changes)
  • Editing or creating a custom extractor class
  • Wiring it into the registry
  • Testing your changes

Audience: pipeline engineers and maintainers.

docs.title: 1) Mental model
docs.text:
• The Extract stage routes each input file to a format-specific extractor using a registry keyed by file extension.
  • An extractor is a small class with:
  • name: a unique string (e.g., "pandoc-md", "oxygen-docx")
  • exts: tuple of handled extensions (e.g., (".md",))
  • extract(src, dst, runner): does the work (often a CLI call) and writes immutable output to dst (DocBook XML).
  • Routing can be overridden at runtime via config.yaml (no code edits) using handler_overrides.

Directory structure (simplified):

dita_etl/
  stages/
    extract.py                # ExtractStage: builds registry, runs in parallel
    extractors/
      base.py                 # FileExtractor protocol (interface)
      md_pandoc.py            # .md → DocBook via Pandoc
      html_pandoc.py          # .html/.htm → DocBook via Pandoc
      docx_pandoc.py          # .docx → DocBook via Pandoc
      docx_oxygen.py          # (optional) .docx → DocBook via Oxygen script

docs.title: 2) Easiest path: switch the extractor in config
docs.text:
If your goal is to change which extractor handles a file type (e.g., use Oxygen for .docx instead of Pandoc), do it in config/config.yaml:

extract:
  handler_overrides:
    ".docx": "oxygen-docx"   # route DOCX to the Oxygen extractor by name
  max_workers: 8             # optional: tune parallelism

Requirements: - The extractor named "oxygen-docx" must be available (e.g., docx_oxygen.py defines class DocxOxygenExtractor: name = "oxygen-docx"). - ExtractStage must pass handler_overrides from config (your orchestrator already does this if you followed prior steps).

Zero code edits. Re-run:

python scripts/cli.py --config config/config.yaml --input /path/to/corpus

docs.title: 3) Modify the existing extractor (“Specify source content”)
docs.text:
If you need to change how an extractor works (arguments, pre/post processing), edit its class.

Example: tweak Pandoc Markdown extractor:

dita_etl/stages/extractors/md_pandoc.py 

from __future__ import annotations
from ...runners import SubprocessRunner

class MdPandocExtractor:
    name = "pandoc-md"
    exts = (".md",)

    def __init__(self, pandoc_path: str):
        self.pandoc = pandoc_path

    def extract(self, src: str, dst: str, runner: SubprocessRunner) -> None:
        # Add filters, metadata, or a reference doc if you want
        args = [
            self.pandoc,
            "-f", "gfm",
            "-t", "docbook",
            # Example additions:
            # "--metadata", "toc=true",
            # "--filter", "/usr/local/bin/pandoc-citeproc",
            src, "-o", dst,
        ]
        runner.run(args)

Rules: - Never modify input files in place. - Always write the complete artifact to dst (DocBook XML). - Keep it deterministic: fixed args, no env randomness.

docs.title: 4) Create a new extractor (custom “Specify source content”)
docs.text: If you’re adding a new source or toolchain, create a new class and register it.

docs.title: 4.1 Create the extractor
docs.text:
dita_etl/stages/extractors/pages_oxygen.py 

from __future__ import annotations
import os
from ...runners import SubprocessRunner

class PagesOxygenExtractor:
    name = "oxygen-pages"
    exts = (".pages",)

    def __init__(self, oxygen_scripts_dir: str):
        if not oxygen_scripts_dir:
            raise ValueError("oxygen_scripts_dir is required for oxygen-pages")
        self.scripts = oxygen_scripts_dir

    def extract(self, src: str, dst: str, runner: SubprocessRunner) -> None:
        # Call a wrapper script that runs Oxygen CLI scenario
        script = os.path.join(self.scripts, "pages2docbook.sh")
        runner.run([script, src, dst])

docs.title: 4.2 Import it so the registry can see it
docs.text:
dita_etl/stages/extractors/__init__.py 

from .base import FileExtractor
from .md_pandoc import MdPandocExtractor
from .html_pandoc import HtmlPandocExtractor
from .docx_pandoc import DocxPandocExtractor
from .docx_oxygen import DocxOxygenExtractor          # if present
from .pages_oxygen import PagesOxygenExtractor        # <-- add this

__all__ = [
    "FileExtractor",
    "MdPandocExtractor",
    "HtmlPandocExtractor",
    "DocxPandocExtractor",
    "DocxOxygenExtractor",
    "PagesOxygenExtractor",
]

The ExtractStage auto-builds the registry from imported classes and applies handler_overrides if provided.

docs.title: 4.3 Route extensions via config
docs.text:
extract:
  handler_overrides:
    ".pages": "oxygen-pages"

docs.title: 5) What to change in ExtractStage (only if needed)
docs.text:
You generally don’t need to touch ExtractStage. If you must, there are two common tweaks:

  • Expose max_workers via config (already supported): 
    ExtractStage(..., max_workers=cfg.extract.get("max_workers"))
  • Add default handlers to the registry build if you want them without overrides. For example, prefer Oxygen for .docx by default: 
    handlers = [
    MdPandocExtractor(self.pandoc_path),
    HtmlPandocExtractor(self.pandoc_path),
    DocxOxygenExtractor(self.oxygen_scripts_dir),  # prefer Oxygen
    DocxPandocExtractor(self.pandoc_path),         # keep fallback
]

docs.title: 6.1 Unit test the extractor args
docs.text:
tests/test_extractors.py already includes patterns like:

def _assert_common_pandoc_shape(call, reader, src, dst):
    assert call[0].endswith("pandoc")
    assert call[1] == "-f" and call[2] == reader
    assert call[3] == "-t" and call[4] == "docbook"
    assert call[-3] == src and call[-2] == "-o" and call[-1] == dst

Add a test for your new extractor (e.g., Oxygen):

def test_pages_oxygen_args(tmp_path, monkeypatch):
    from dita_etl.stages.extractors.pages_oxygen import PagesOxygenExtractor
    from dita_etl.runners import SubprocessRunner

    class DummyRunner(SubprocessRunner):
        def __init__(self): self.calls=[]
        def run(self, args): self.calls.append(args); Path(args[-1]).parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True); Path(args[-1]).write_text("<docbook/>")

    runner = DummyRunner()
    ext = PagesOxygenExtractor(oxygen_scripts_dir="/opt/oxygen/scripts")
    src = str(tmp_path / "x.pages"); dst = str(tmp_path / "out.xml")
    Path(src).write_text("stub", encoding="utf-8")

    ext.extract(src, dst, runner)

    call = runner.calls[0]
    assert "/opt/oxygen/scripts/pages2docbook.sh" in call[0]
    assert call[-3] == src and call[-1] == dst
    assert Path(dst).exists()

docs.title: 6.2 Registry routing & overrides
docs.text:
Use/extend the existing test_pipeline.py: - Verify .pages routes to oxygen-pages when set in handler_overrides. - Ensure per-file failures are captured but don’t abort the entire run.

Run: 

pytest -q

docs.title: 7) Operational checklist
docs.text:
• Determinism: fixed CLI args, no time-based output, same inputs → same DocBook.
  • Immutability: never mutate sources; always fully write dst.
  • Graceful failure: raise a clear SubprocessError so ExtractStage captures it per file and continues.
  • Config-first: expose all variable aspects (script paths, flags) via config.yaml.
  • Docs: update README/design docs with the extractor’s name, supported exts, and any prerequisites (e.g., Oxygen CLI).
  • Perf: tune extract.max_workers if you see I/O saturation or tool concurrency limits.

docs.title: 8) Common pitfalls (and fixes)
docs.text:
• Relative imports: extractors live at dita_etl/stages/extractors/; import the runner with from ...runners import SubprocessRunner (three dots).
  • Registry not seeing your extractor: make sure it’s imported in extractors/__init__.py.
  • Overriding doesn’t work: the name in your extractor must match the string in handler_overrides exactly.
  • Silent failures: if your extractor swallows exceptions or doesn’t raise on non-zero exit codes, ExtractStage can’t quarantine properly. Always raise SubprocessError (the default SubprocessRunner does this for you).

docs.title: 9) Quick reference
docs.text:
• Add/modify extractor → dita_etl/stages/extractors/*.py
  • Register/import → dita_etl/stages/extractors/__init__.py
  • Route by config → config/config.yaml → extract.handler_overrides
  • Parallelism → extract.max_workers or ExtractStage(max_workers=...)
  • Tests → tests/test_extractors.py, tests/test_pipeline.py

That’s it. With the registry + overrides in place, changing the “Specify source content” extractor is mostly a config flip, and deeper changes are isolated to a single, tiny class.

docs.title: Framework for Evaluating ETL Processes (with DITA Conversion Context)
Introduction
docs.text: Extract-Transform-Load (ETL) processes are the backbone of data and content pipelines, requiring robust design and careful governance to ensure reliability. This framework presents six core principles for evaluating any ETL process, with an emphasis on DITA conversion pipelines (converting documentation to/from DITA format) as a practical example. The principles – Isolate Concerns, Contracts over Tools, Deterministic Outputs, Config-First, Graceful Degradation, and Testability – serve as a checklist for engineers, team leads, and auditors to assess pipeline quality. Each principle is described below with rationale, best practices, and a checklist of evaluation criteria. By adhering to these guidelines, teams can build ETL/DITA conversion workflows that are maintainable, reproducible, and resilient in the face of change.

docs.title: 1. Isolate Concerns: Single-Responsibility Stages & Immutable Artifacts
docs.text:
Principle: Each ETL stage should have a single, well-defined responsibility. Data flows through clearly defined inputs and outputs, and intermediate artifacts are immutable (never modified in place). This separation of concerns makes the pipeline easier to understand, test, and maintain.

Rationale: Breaking a pipeline into independent components (extractors, transformers, loaders, etc.) prevents a “monolithic” design that is hard to debug and scale. When each module does one job, failures are contained and do not cascade across the entire pipeline oai_citation:0‡brainforge.ai. Teams can develop and deploy stages in parallel, and each stage can be optimized or scaled independently oai_citation:1‡brainforge.ai. Immutability of outputs ensures that once a stage produces data, that data isn’t altered later by side effects – improving reproducibility and simplifying error recovery.

Key Practices: - Single Responsibility: Design each stage (or microservice) to perform one function (e.g., file parse, data normalization, format conversion). Example: In a DITA conversion, have separate stages for extracting source content, converting to DITA XML, and generating output formats (HTML/PDF) rather than one big script. - Explicit I/O: Define the input and output schema or file format for every stage. Avoid hidden side-effects or implicit data passing. Each module reads its input from a well-known interface (file, API, message) and writes output in a standard form for the next stage. - Immutable Artifacts: Never modify an output artifact after it’s written. If data needs changing, create a new version. For example, rather than updating a database table or XML file in place, write to a new table or file. This create-new-then-replace pattern eliminates many consistency issues oai_citation:2‡datakitchen.io. In practice, this could mean writing transformed DITA files to a new directory or using versioned file names instead of overwriting existing ones. - Loose Coupling: Because each stage has its own outputs, upstream and downstream stages remain decoupled. One stage can be swapped or updated with minimal impact on others (as long as it respects the agreed input/output contract).

Evaluation Checklist – Isolate Concerns: - Is each ETL stage responsible for a single logical task, without conflating multiple concerns? - Are the inputs and outputs of each stage well-defined (e.g., via schemas or interface specs) and easily identifiable by other components? - When a stage produces data, is that data kept immutable thereafter (no in-place edits or partial updates)? - If a failure occurs in one stage, does it only affect that stage (i.e. other stages can still run or be retried independently) oai_citation:3‡brainforge.ai? - Can you replace or refactor one component without needing to rewrite the entire pipeline (indicative of loose coupling)?

docs.title: 2. Contracts over Tools: Schema-Driven Interfaces, Not Ad-Hoc Integrations
docs.text:
Principle: Pipeline components should communicate via documented data contracts (schemas, APIs, file formats) rather than through undocumented, tool-specific behaviors. Emphasize clear interfaces and data definitions over tightly integrating via library internals or shared in-memory objects.

Rationale: Treating the data schema as a contract between stages ensures that each component operates on agreed structure and semantics, independent of the tooling used to produce or consume the data. This prevents accidental tight coupling due to using the same library or in-memory data structures, which can lead to brittle pipelines (e.g., breakage when a library is updated or replaced). Instead, each stage should rely on the explicit schema of the data – for example, a JSON schema, XML DTD/Schema (for DITA), or database table definition. Well-documented schemas act as a blueprint and “source of truth” for the data being exchanged oai_citation:4‡confluent.io, enabling different tools or implementations to plug into the pipeline as long as they honor the contract.

Key Practices: - Define Schemas/Contracts: For each data interchange point, define the schema (fields, types, allowed values) and make it version-controlled documentation. In a DITA pipeline, this might include the DITA XML schema for topics or maps, and any extended structure or metadata your process requires. Downstream code should rely on this schema, not on how a particular library represents the data in memory. - Use Standard Formats: Prefer open, standard data formats (CSV, JSON, XML, Avro, etc.) with known schemas for interchange. For instance, if one stage outputs a list of DITA topics, using well-formed XML or JSON with a published schema allows another stage (maybe written in a different language or using a different XML parser) to consume it reliably. - Decouple from Implementation: Don’t require that Stage B uses the same programming language or framework as Stage A. Instead, require that Stage B can read Stage A’s output format. This focuses integration on data contracts (what the data looks like) rather than code or tool specifics. Loosely coupled stages bound by data contracts can be developed and updated independently oai_citation:5‡confluent.io. - Document and Version Contracts: Maintain documentation for each interface (e.g., using API docs or schema files). Version them when changes are made, and ensure backward compatibility or migration plans for any contract changes so downstream components don’t break unexpectedly.

Evaluation Checklist – Contracts over Tools: - Are data structures exchanged between stages defined by a schema or formal contract (as opposed to implicit knowledge or tied to a specific library’s objects)? - Are these schemas documented and version-controlled (e.g., JSON/YAML schema files, API interface documents, DITA DTDs)? - Can different tools or languages be used for upstream vs. downstream stages without breaking the pipeline (i.e., is the integration format/tool-agnostic)? - When changes occur (new fields, changes in data structure), is there a clear contract versioning and migration strategy to prevent consumer breakage? - Do team members and stakeholders have access to clear documentation of each stage’s input/output data models?

docs.title: 3. Deterministic Outputs: Reproducibility and Idempotence
docs.text:
Principle: The pipeline must be deterministic – given the same inputs and configuration, it produces the same outputs every time. Every transformation should be pure (free of unintended side effects), and rerunning the pipeline (or any stage) on the same data should not introduce variation or duplication. In practice, making each step idempotent and even hashing outputs for verification helps ensure this determinism.

Rationale: Deterministic behavior is critical for trust in the pipeline’s results. It means that results are reproducible and differences in output truly indicate differences in input or logic, not randomness or external timing issues. Idempotent operations (“same result whether run once or multiple times” oai_citation:6‡brainforge.ai) are especially important in ETL, because pipelines often need retries or re-processing of past data. If a job fails halfway and is re-run, it should not double-load data or produce different results. Determinism also aids debugging and auditing: one can trace a specific output back to the exact input that produced it, and be confident the transformation is consistent. In regulated or mission-critical contexts (like publishing technical documents via DITA conversion), determinism ensures that content updates are predictable and free of surprises.

Key Practices: - Pure Transformations: Design transformation logic to avoid external non-deterministic factors like current timestamps, random generators, or order-dependent operations without sorting. For example, if converting DITA to HTML, don’t insert a “generated on DATE” timestamp without a controlled override, or the output will differ each run. If randomness is needed (e.g., sampling data), use a fixed seed or input-driven key. - Idempotent Writes: When loading data to a target, use approaches that naturally handle duplicates or retries gracefully. A common method is upsert or complete overwrite rather than incremental append oai_citation:7‡brainforge.ai. For instance, if the pipeline is loading converted DITA files to a CMS, it could replace the entire content or update existing entries by ID, so running it twice doesn’t create duplicate entries. - Same Inputs, Same Outputs: As a rule, verify that running the ETL/convert process on identical input yields identical output files or database state. This can be enforced by checksums or hashes. Consider computing a hash of each output dataset or file and comparing it to a stored “golden” hash from a prior run. Modern data versioning tools use this technique: for example, Data Version Control (DVC) computes a cryptographic hash of data files such that any change, however minor, will cause the hash to change oai_citation:8‡medium.com. Incorporating hash checks at each stage can quickly flag unintended differences in supposedly unchanged outputs. - Deterministic Ordering: If the pipeline aggregates or merges data from multiple sources, enforce a stable sorting or ordering before output. This way, two runs don’t differ just because of order of processing. For DITA content, this might mean always processing files in a certain order or normalizing how XML attributes are ordered when serializing. - Avoid Side Effects: A stage should not depend on hidden global state or produce side effects that persist beyond that stage (aside from its defined outputs). E.g., if a transformation stage reads from an external service (not in input data), network hiccups or different response times could introduce nondeterminism. Prefer to include all needed data in the inputs or use cached responses so the stage’s behavior is consistent.

Evaluation Checklist – Deterministic Outputs: - If you run the entire pipeline twice with the same source data and config, do you get byte-for-byte identical outputs (barring intentionally variable fields like timestamps)? - Are transformations free from reliance on system time, random seeds (unless fixed), or external unordered data sources that could vary between runs? - Can each stage be retried or re-run on the same input without producing duplicate records or otherwise corrupting the results oai_citation:9‡brainforge.ai? (E.g., running the load stage twice won’t insert the same data twice.) - Do you implement output verification, such as comparing output hashes or record counts to expected values, to detect any divergence on re-runs? - In case of partial failures and restarts, does the pipeline properly resume without skipping or repeating data unintentionally (indicative of proper checkpointing and idempotence)?

docs.title: 4. Config-First: Externalized Configuration for Mappings & Rules
docs.text:
Principle: Configuration, not code, should drive variable aspects of the pipeline. All mappings, business rules, taxonomies, chunking rules, and output format specifications should live in external, versioned config files (YAML, JSON, XML, etc.). The code of the pipeline then becomes a generic engine that can be tuned or re-purposed by simply changing configs, rather than rewriting logic.

Rationale: A config-first approach separates what the pipeline does (defined by configs) from how it does it (the generic code). This yields flexibility: non-developers or power users can adjust behavior by editing config files, and the pipeline can handle new scenarios by loading new configs. It also promotes consistency and version control for pipeline behavior. By checking in configuration files to source control, changes to ETL behavior (like a new field mapping or content filter) are tracked just like code changes, facilitating reviews and rollbacks. In DITA conversion, for example, one might have a YAML file defining how DITA elements map to output HTML elements, or which metadata taxonomies to apply – changing the YAML updates the conversion rules without altering code. Modern ETL practices refer to this as metadata-driven or declarative pipeline design oai_citation:10‡gisrrj.com.

Key Practices: - External Config Files: Store things like field mappings, data type conversions, filtering rules, and output formatting options in configuration files. For DITA, you might externalize a list of element names to exclude, or a JSON mapping of conditional text settings for different output flavors. Ensure the pipeline reads these at runtime (or build time) and does not have these values hardcoded. - Version Control & Environments: Keep configs in Git (or similar). If different environments (dev/staging/prod) need different settings (e.g., database connection strings or a flag to include draft content in conversion), use separate config files or a parameterized config, rather than if/else in code. Changes to config are changes to pipeline behavior – they should undergo the same review and testing as code changes. - Schema for Config: Treat config files themselves as data with a schema. For instance, define a JSON schema for your config or at least document all the fields in it. This makes it easier to validate config files and for new engineers to understand how to adjust them. - Examples of Config-First: In the pipeline code, instead of a function that explicitly lists how to transform each field, the code could loop over fields defined in a config structure (mapping source field names to target names/types). In DITA processing, instead of code that explicitly checks for every possible DITA element, a config file could list the elements that should be transformed in certain ways (like a mapping of DITA element -> transformation function or style), so supporting a new element is as simple as adding to the config. The pipeline becomes data-driven, adapting to different input schemas or output requirements through config changes oai_citation:11‡gisrrj.com. - Consistency and Reuse: Config-first design often allows the same pipeline code to be reused for different projects or data sources by swapping configs. This is highly valuable for standardizing processes. E.g., one DITA conversion pipeline could handle multiple document types by selecting different config files for each type’s rules.

Evaluation Checklist – Config-First: - Are dynamic aspects of the ETL (mappings, rules, parameters) pulled from config files rather than hardcoded? (Inspect a sample of the code – do you see many literal values or conditional logic that could be in config instead?) - Are configuration files for the pipeline present, human-readable (YAML/JSON/etc.), and stored in version control alongside code? oai_citation:12‡brainforge.ai - If a new data source or format needs to be onboarded, can it be done by adding/updating a config file (with minimal code changes)? For example, adding a new DITA element transformation by editing a mapping file. - Do config changes undergo a review/deployment process, and are they documented or validated (to avoid typos breaking the pipeline)? - Does the pipeline clearly separate configuration loading from execution logic, indicating a clean contract between “config defines behavior” and “code executes behavior”?

docs.title: 5. Graceful Degradation: Fault Tolerance & Partial Processing
docs.text:
Principle: The ETL pipeline should continue to deliver useful output even if parts of the input data are malformed or certain components fail. Graceful degradation means designing for partial success: one bad record (or one failed sub-task) does not halt the entire pipeline. Instead, problematic data is isolated (quarantined) for later review, and the pipeline completes for the rest. “Don’t let perfect be the enemy of good data.”

Rationale: In large-scale or heterogeneous data processing, it’s common to encounter errors – a corrupt file, a schema anomaly, a few records that violate assumptions. If the pipeline is all-or-nothing, a minor issue can result in no data delivered at all, which may be unacceptable for the business. A resilient pipeline anticipates errors and handles them so that non-affected data still flows through oai_citation:13‡brainforge.ai. This is especially important in content conversion (e.g., DITA) where one invalid topic should not prevent thousands of other topics from publishing. By quarantining bad records (often using a “dead letter queue” or error log), the system provides a safety valve: good data is delivered, bad data is set aside for investigation without stopping the entire run oai_citation:14‡brainforge.ai. This principle improves reliability and trust – stakeholders get partial results with clear indication of any omissions, rather than no results at all.

Key Practices: - Error Handling on Record Level: Implement robust try/catch or validation around processing of individual records or units. If one item fails to transform, catch the exception, log the error (with the record identifier and reason), and mark that record as failed without crashing the whole pipeline thread. For example, if one DITA file is invalid XML, skip it (move it to a “failed” folder) and continue with the next file, while recording that error in a report. - Dead Letter Queues / Quarantine: Have a mechanism to collect bad data separately. In streaming pipelines, a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) is a common pattern – a separate output where messages that couldn’t be processed are sent oai_citation:15‡brainforge.ai. In batch ETL or file processing, this could be a “quarantine” directory or a database table for rejected records. This allows you to examine and reprocess these later, without blocking the main data flow oai_citation:16‡brainforge.ai. - Partial Extraction/Load Strategies: Design extract and load processes to handle partial data gracefully. For instance, if extracting from multiple sources and one source is down, the pipeline could still load data from the others and just flag the missing source. Or if 1000 records are extracted and 5 fail validation, 995 should still be transformed and loaded. Tools or frameworks that support checkpointing and retry with skip help here – e.g., you might configure an ETL job to continue after X errors, or use circuit breakers to isolate failing sub-tasks. - Flag and Continue: Wherever possible, prefer warning + continue over immediate failure for non-critical errors. Of course, truly critical failures (like no connection to the main database) might halt the run, but the principle is to narrow the scope of what’s “fatal.” For each stage, ask: Can it emit a partial result if some inputs are bad? In DITA conversion, maybe a few topics fail to convert – the system could still publish the converted ones and list which failed, rather than abort the entire publication. - Graceful Degradation in Action: As an example, Coefficient (a data integration platform) implements this principle by completing imports with available data and flagging problematic records when only a partial extraction succeeds oai_citation:17‡coefficient.io. The Snowflake warehouse still receives as much data as possible, while errors are noted. This approach ensures critical information is delivered on time, and errors can be handled afterward, aligning with high availability practices.

Evaluation Checklist – Graceful Degradation: - Does the pipeline have built-in error handling for individual records or sub-tasks (as opposed to failing on the first error)? (Check for try/catch logic around processing loops, etc.) - Is there a mechanism (DLQ, error file, quarantine table) to capture and isolate records that failed processing, for later review? oai_citation:18‡brainforge.ai - If a non-critical component or data source is unavailable, can the pipeline skip it and still process the rest? (E.g., skip one source’s data, or fill with defaults, rather than total failure.) - Are there configurable thresholds for errors? (For instance, “if more than 5% of records fail, then stop; otherwise continue” – this prevents infinite tolerance but allows small error rates.) - Upon completion of a run that had some errors, does the pipeline output include clear notifications or logs about what was skipped and why, so that the issue can be addressed? (Transparency in graceful degradation is key – silent failures are dangerous oai_citation:19‡medium.com oai_citation:20‡medium.com.)

docs.title: 6. Testability: Fixtures, Golden Files, and Automated Tests for Every Stage
docs.text:
Principle: Treat the ETL pipeline as production code – each component should be testable in isolation, with automated tests using realistic sample data (fixtures) and expected outputs (golden files or baseline snapshots). Testing isn’t an afterthought but an integral part of pipeline design. Every transformation or load step should have a known correct output for a given input, against which the implementation can be validated.

Rationale: Data pipelines often evolve and grow more complex; without proper tests, changes can introduce subtle data errors that might go unnoticed until they reach end-users. By creating fixtures (sample input datasets or files) and golden files (the expected output for those inputs), developers and auditors can verify that changes to the pipeline do not break existing functionality or data integrity. Golden files serve as an absolute source of truth for expected output oai_citation:21‡medium.com – if the pipeline output deviates, either there’s a bug or the golden file needs to be updated (after careful review). This principle ensures regression testing is possible: as new features are added, the core transformations are continually validated. Moreover, testability implies modular design (which we have via isolated concerns) – you can run a stage on test data in a local environment or CI pipeline. For leads and auditors, having a suite of pipeline tests increases confidence in data quality and makes onboarding new team members easier (they can run tests to see how things are supposed to work).

Key Practices: - Unit Tests for Transformations: Write unit tests for individual transformation functions or modules. For example, if there’s a function to map legacy XML to DITA elements, feed it a small XML snippet as a fixture and assert it produces the correct DITA XML fragment. This isolates logic in a controlled setting. - Integration Tests for Stages: Create end-to-end tests for each stage using a fixture input dataset and comparing the entire output to a stored golden file. For instance, have a sample source file (or database dump) and run the extract stage, then compare the output to a golden extract file. Similarly test transform and load stages. Using file diff or hash comparisons against golden outputs can quickly flag discrepancies. Golden files are essentially the expected output data saved from a known good run oai_citation:22‡medium.com. They make validation simple: if the output byte-for-byte matches the golden file, the test passes. If not, something changed – which could be intentional (then update the golden) or a bug. - End-to-End Testing Environment: Establish a pipeline test environment where the entire ETL can run on a reduced scale (e.g., a subset of data or synthetic data). This could be a staging database or just in-memory data. The idea is to simulate the whole pipeline and verify it runs to completion and produces correct aggregated results. In a DITA conversion scenario, an end-to-end test might start from a source documentation set and end with published output, verifying that certain known documents convert correctly and appear in the output. - Automated Testing & CI: Integrate these tests into a continuous integration (CI) system oai_citation:23‡brainforge.ai. Every code or config change should trigger pipeline tests – unit tests for logic and possibly sample data integration tests. This catches issues early. The test suite should include checks not only for correctness of transformation but also data quality constraints (e.g., no missing required fields after transform, or number of records matches expectation). Modern data testing frameworks like Great Expectations or dbt’s test features can automate some of these checks within the pipeline oai_citation:24‡brainforge.ai oai_citation:25‡brainforge.ai. - Fixtures and Reusable Test Data: Maintain a library of fixture inputs that cover typical and edge cases (small, focused data that triggers certain logic branches, as well as full-size samples). For DITA, you might have a fixture DITA file that includes edge cases (special characters, conditional text, etc.) to ensure the pipeline handles them as expected. - Golden File Updates and Reviews: When pipeline logic intentionally changes (say, we improved the transformation logic and the expected output legitimately changes), update the golden files using a controlled process: run the pipeline on the fixture, review the diff of the old vs new output, and if acceptable, commit the new golden files. This way, output changes are always reviewed.

Evaluation Checklist – Testability: - Does each stage or major component of the pipeline have corresponding tests (unit or integration) with defined expected outcomes? (For example, ask to see the test files or evidence of their existence – e.g., a tests/ directory in the repository.) - Are there golden files or baseline outputs stored for comparison? oai_citation:26‡medium.com (This indicates that expected results have been captured and are used for regression testing.) - Can the pipeline be run in a non-production environment on sample data end-to-end? (This implies an easily deployable pipeline, perhaps using configuration to point to a test database or local files.) - Is there a continuous integration setup that automatically runs pipeline tests on changes? oai_citation:27‡brainforge.ai - Have edge cases and error conditions been considered in testing? (E.g., tests for what happens when a record is malformed – does the test pipeline skip it and log it as expected?) - For DITA-specific processes: are there validation tests ensuring the converted output conforms to DITA standards or business rules (possibly using DITA-OT validators or schema checks as part of testing)?

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
By evaluating an ETL process against these six principles – from modular design and clear data contracts to reproducible outputs, configurable behavior, graceful error handling, and rigorous testing – organizations can ensure their data pipelines (and DITA document conversion workflows) are robust and maintainable. This framework functions like a quality checklist for pipeline design and audits. It encourages separation of concerns and strong interfaces for agility, deterministic and idempotent processing for consistency, externalized configurations for flexibility, fault tolerance for reliability, and thorough testing for confidence. Adhering to these practices ultimately leads to pipelines that deliver correct data (or content) on time, withstand changes in source or requirements, and instill trust in the engineering team as well as the pipeline’s stakeholders. Adopting this mindset in your ETL/DITA conversion projects will significantly reduce firefighting and increase the reliability of your data-driven operations.

References (selected): Principles and examples drawn from industry best practices and literature, including modular pipeline architecture oai_citation:28‡brainforge.ai, data contracts via schema registry oai_citation:29‡confluent.io, idempotent processing for reproducibility oai_citation:30‡brainforge.ai, metadata-driven ETL design oai_citation:31‡gisrrj.com, graceful degradation patterns oai_citation:32‡brainforge.ai oai_citation:33‡coefficient.io, and pipeline testing techniques oai_citation:34‡brainforge.ai oai_citation:35‡medium.com which reinforce the guidelines above.

docs.title: ETL Pipeline for Converting Multiple Formats to DITA 1.3
Introduction
docs.text:
Converting a wide variety of document formats into DITA 1.3 XML can be achieved with an Extract-Transform-Load (ETL) pipeline that leverages Oxygen XML Editor (and its CLI scripting capabilities) alongside tools like Pandoc for broad format support. This pipeline will take inputs such as Markdown, Microsoft Word (Docx), Apple Pages, reStructuredText, LaTeX, EPUB, HTML, and many others, and produce structured DITA content (Concept, Task, and Reference topics) assembled into DITA maps. The solution is designed with robust software engineering principles in mind:

  • Isolated concerns: The conversion process is broken into clear stages (extract, transform, load). Each stage has a single responsibility with explicit inputs/outputs, and intermediate artifacts are treated as immutable (never modified in-place).
  • Contracts over tools: Stages communicate via well-defined formats and schemas (e.g. DocBook XML or XHTML as intermediates, and DITA XML as output) rather than through ad-hoc in-memory structures. This ensures any component (Pandoc, XSLT, etc.) can be swapped as long as it respects the input/output schema contracts.
  • Deterministic output: Given the same inputs and config, the pipeline will always produce the same DITA output. Each step’s results can be content-hashed to verify repeatability oai_citation:0‡medium.com. This determinism helps with caching and debugging (if a step is re-run with identical input, a hash match can skip unnecessary processing).
  • Configuration-first: All mappings, taxonomies, chunking rules, and output options are driven by external config files (YAML/JSON) under version control. No magic constants are hard-coded – things like “which styles map to Task topics” or “how to split a file into multiple topics” are defined in config, making the process transparent and adjustable without code changes.
  • Graceful degradation: The pipeline is resilient to conversion failures. A problematic file or section will not halt the entire run – instead it will be quarantined for review while the pipeline continues oai_citation:1‡medium.com. For example, if one document cannot be fully converted (due to an unsupported format feature or a parsing error), that error is logged and the pipeline moves on with the rest, ensuring partial extraction doesn’t block overall progress.
  • Testable components: Each stage of the pipeline is designed to be independently testable. We maintain small sample inputs (fixtures) and expected “golden” output files for unit testing of each stage. This means we can run a stage in isolation (e.g. conversion of a Markdown snippet to intermediate XML) and compare it to the expected result, giving confidence that changes in the pipeline don’t introduce regressions.

Using Python on macOS as the orchestrator, we can integrate Oxygen’s powerful XML processing (via its CLI or Saxon XSLT engine) with Pandoc’s broad format conversion capabilities. The end result will be batch-automated conversion and classification of documents into DITA 1.3 XML, complete with DITA maps, and with support for custom DITA DTD specializations if needed.

docs.title: Pipeline Overview
docs.text:
Extract-Transform-Load Stages: 1. Extraction (Input Conversion): Parse and convert source files from their native formats into a normalized intermediate representation. This stage handles a wide array of input formats – from lightweight markups (Markdown, reStructuredText, AsciiDoc, Org-Mode, etc.) to binary docs (Word .docx, RTF, ODT), HTML/XML formats, wikis, slide decks, and more – using appropriate tools. The goal is to produce a consistent XML or JSON structure from all inputs, easing downstream processing. 2. Transformation (Mapping to DITA): Take the intermediate representation and transform it into DITA XML topics. This involves classifying and splitting content into DITA topic types (Concept, Task, Reference) and ensuring each topic follows the DITA 1.3 structure. It may use XSLT (with Saxon via Oxygen) or Python logic to map elements to DITA equivalents. This stage also assembles metadata and applies any taxonomies or mappings defined in the config (for example, ensuring certain sections become <steps> in a Task topic, etc.). 3. Load (Assembly and Output): Save the resulting DITA topics to the desired output location and assemble a DITA map (.ditamap) that organizes all topics. The DITA map can reflect an outline or hierarchy (derived from the source structure or config rules) and ensures all topics are referenced. In this stage, we also perform final validation (using Oxygen’s DITA validation or DITA-OT checks) to verify that the output is compliant with the DITA DTD (including any custom specializations). Successfully converted files become part of the output set, while any files that were quarantined or failed conversion are documented in a report for later manual attention.

Each stage reads from and writes to well-defined files, and by chaining these stages we achieve an end-to-end conversion. The entire pipeline can be run as a Python script (or within an orchestration framework) to handle batches of documents automatically.

Below, we detail each stage and the tools/plugins involved, aligning with the guiding principles.

docs.title: Stage 1: Extraction – Multi-Format Input Conversion
docs.text:
Objective: Ingest source files of various formats and convert them into a uniform intermediate format (or a small set of formats) for easier processing. This isolates the complexity of dealing with dozens of formats into one stage.

  • Broad Format Support via Pandoc: We utilize Pandoc as a universal converter to handle the “widest number of file types.” Pandoc is renowned for supporting a broad array of input and output formats. In fact, Pandoc’s library can read/write markdown (CommonMark, GFM), reStructuredText, Asciidoc, Org-mode, Emacs Wiki, Textile, txt2tags, Djot; HTML4/5; EPUB (v2/v3); FictionBook FB2; GNU TexInfo; roff (man, ms); LaTeX; ConTeXt; DocBook (v4/v5); JATS; TEI; OpenDocument XML; OPML; BibTeX/BibLaTeX; CSL JSON/YAML; RIS; Word .docx; RTF; ODT; Jupyter .ipynb; InDesign ICML; various wiki markups (MediaWiki, DokuWiki, XWiki, Jira, etc.); slideshow formats (PowerPoint, Beamer, reveal.js, etc.); CSV/TSV; and even PDF (via external tools) oai_citation:2‡hackage.haskell.org oai_citation:3‡hackage.haskell.org. This means nearly all the formats listed in the question can be ingested by Pandoc. For example: a Markdown file or an Org-Mode note can be parsed, a Word .docx or RTF document can be read, a LaTeX or ConTeXt file can be parsed, etc. Using Pandoc’s command-line in a Python subprocess (or the pypandoc library), we convert each input file to a chosen intermediate format such as DocBook XML or HTML5. Both DocBook and XHTML are XML formats with schemas, serving as “contracts” for the next stage.

  • Using Oxygen’s Batch Converter for Certain Formats: For some formats, we can leverage Oxygen XML Editor’s Batch Converter add-on via its CLI scripts for direct conversion to DITA or XML. Oxygen’s scripting toolkit (requires a scripting license) provides a batchConverter script that can convert HTML->DITA, Markdown->DITA, Word->DITA, Excel->DITA, Confluence->DITA, DocBook->DITA, among others oai_citation:4‡oxygenxml.com oai_citation:5‡oxygenxml.com. For instance, if a .docx (Word) file or Markdown file needs conversion, we could call Oxygen’s batch converter (via a shell call from Python) to get DITA XML directly. However, in this pipeline we might prefer converting to a neutral format like DocBook first (which preserves semantics better) and doing the DITA mapping in the next stage. The Oxygen converter is still useful for quick conversion or for formats Pandoc might not handle (e.g. an Excel to DITA table conversion).

  • Handling Binary/Proprietary Formats: Some formats (e.g. Apple Pages) are not directly supported by Pandoc or Oxygen. For these, the pipeline should include a pre-processing step: for example, Apple Pages documents can be programmatically exported to .docx or .pdf (using AppleScript or the Pages GUI) before feeding into the pipeline. Similarly, if a format is unsupported, document it in the logs and skip or convert via an intermediate step (graceful degradation).

  • Explicit I/O and Immutable Artifacts: Each input file is processed independently, and the output of this stage is an intermediate file (or files) stored in an “extraction output” directory. For example, Guide123.docx might be converted to Guide123.xml in DocBook format. These intermediate files are treated as read-only once written (immutable); if the pipeline runs again on the same input without changes, it will produce the exact same intermediate (ensured by deterministic conversion settings). We can even generate a hash (e.g. MD5 or SHA256) of the content of each intermediate file and store it – both for caching (to skip re-conversion if the source hasn’t changed) and for traceability (each intermediate artifact can be traced and verified).

  • Logging and Quarantining Errors: If the conversion of a particular file fails or yields low-confidence output, this stage will log the error and mark that file for review. For example, if Pandoc encounters an unsupported construct in a LaTeX file, we capture Pandoc’s error message. Rather than aborting, the script continues with the next file (unless the failure is critical). Files that couldn’t be converted (or with partial conversion) are moved to a “quarantine” list. We output any content that was parsed before the failure, if possible, and annotate it so it doesn’t break the pipeline. This quarantine strategy ensures the pipeline keeps running with valid data separated from invalid, instead of failing entirely oai_citation:6‡medium.com.

By the end of Stage 1, we have a collection of intermediate XML/JSON files representing the original documents in a consistent structure. We also have metadata (like the original file name, source format, timestamps, etc.) to carry forward. This stage’s isolation means that everything downstream works with a uniform contract (e.g. DocBook XML), simplifying the next transformations.

docs.title: Stage 2: Transformation – Classifying and Converting to DITA Topics
docs.text:
Objective: Transform the intermediate representation into DITA 1.3-compliant XML topics, applying the rules for concept/task/reference typing, chunking content into topics, and enforcing structural consistency. This is the heart of the ETL process, where content is semantically structured into DITA.

  • XSLT Mapping (Contract-Driven): Given our intermediate is XML (e.g. DocBook 5 or XHTML), an XSLT stylesheet can be used to systematically map elements to DITA equivalents. For example, DocBook <section> or HTML <h1> might become a DITA <topic>. We will develop XSLT transformations (using Saxon, the XSLT engine integrated with Oxygen) to handle this mapping. The XSL defines the contract between intermediate schema and DITA schema – e.g., how a DocBook <para> becomes a <p> in DITA, how a DocBook <informaltable> becomes a DITA <table>, etc. This approach keeps us tool-agnostic; if a different converter produced the same DocBook, the XSLT would still work (adhering to “contracts over tools”). Oxygen’s Batch Converter actually includes a ready-made XSLT for DocBook to DITA conversion oai_citation:7‡oxygenxml.com, which we can reuse or adapt within our pipeline. Using Oxygen’s CLI, we could invoke a pre-configured transformation scenario for DocBook->DITA, or run Saxon via command-line in the Python script to apply our custom XSL.

  • Topic Chunking Rules: Often one source document needs to be split into multiple DITA topics (to avoid monster topics and to fit DITA’s topic-based authoring paradigm). We implement chunking rules defined in the config. For example, the config might specify that top-level sections (<h1> in HTML or <chapter> in DocBook) become separate DITA topic files, and nested sections (<h2>, <h3> etc.) become nested topics or sections within the parent topic. In Markdown-based DITA authoring, each level-1 heading starts a new topic oai_citation:8‡dita-ot.org, and we can mirror that logic: our XSLT can generate a new <topic> element each time it encounters a top-level section, closing the previous one. The output might be a single DITA file with nested <topic> elements (which DITA allows), or multiple .dita files – depending on how we prefer to output them. A mapping config can control this, for instance: chunk_level: 1 could mean “split at first-level headings into distinct files.” All decisions are externalized to config so that adjusting chunking doesn’t require code changes.

  • Classification into Concept/Task/Reference: A key requirement is to route content into Concept, Task, or Reference topic types appropriately. DITA 1.3 has these base types: Concept (for conceptual/background info), Task (for procedural step-by-step instructions), and Reference (for factual or reference data like specs) oai_citation:9‡heretto.com. We use both config rules and content cues to classify topics:

  • Config-driven classification: The simplest method is to let the user/config specify the intended topic type per document or section. For example, the YAML config could list file patterns or section titles that map to a certain type (e.g., any document with “How to” in its title is a Task). Another config approach is by location: e.g., all files in a Procedures/ folder could default to Task. These mappings are read in at runtime and applied during transformation.

  • Pattern recognition: In addition, the transformation stage can inspect content to infer type. For instance, if in the intermediate we detect an ordered list of steps (1., 2., 3.) or words like “Step 1:” or presence of imperative verbs, that section likely represents a procedure -> generate a <task> topic with proper <steps> inside. Conversely, if a section contains a table of parameters or a list of reference facts, it might be a Reference topic. If it’s mostly descriptive paragraphs with no obvious steps, it’s probably Concept. These heuristics can be refined over time or even augmented with a simple ML classifier, but starting with regex/keyword rules is often effective.

  • Applying the classification: Once we determine a topic’s type, the XSLT will generate the appropriate root element (<concept>, <task>, or <reference> instead of a generic <topic>). The DITA-OT Markdown mechanism, for example, uses a convention where adding a {.task} class to a Markdown H1 triggers a Task topic in output oai_citation:10‡dita-ot.org. In our XSLT, we effectively replicate that outcome: e.g., if a topic is marked as Task, we produce the <task> element and inside it a <taskbody> containing <steps>, etc., structured according to the DITA Task model. We ensure that required subelements (like a Task’s <title>, <steps>, and at least one <step> with <cmd>) are populated. Similarly, Reference topics might consist of property tables or definition lists formatted into DITA <dl> or tables. Concept topics would have a simpler <section> structure under a <concept> body. The Heretto DITA guide summarizes it well: Concept topics present background info, Task topics provide procedural how-to instructions, and Reference topics contain detailed factual information (often tabular or list form) oai_citation:11‡heretto.com.

  • Preserving and Mapping Content Elements: Besides overall structure, the transform stage maps finer details: paragraphs, lists, tables, images, code blocks, etc., into appropriate DITA elements (while obeying DITA content models). For instance:

  • Paragraphs become <p> within the DITA topic body or within <context> or other sections as appropriate. The first paragraph might become a <shortdesc> if config indicates that (DITA concept and task allow a short description element).
  • Section headings could become <section><title>...</title> blocks inside concept/reference topics, or in tasks they might map to <context>, <result>, etc., if the config or content suggests (e.g., a heading "Results" could map to the <result> section of a Task topic type).
  • Ordered lists that appear to be procedural steps: if classified as a Task, we wrap them in <steps><step>…</step></steps> and put the action in a <cmd> element inside each step (DITA’s way of marking what the user does) and any explanatory text in <info> oai_citation:12‡dita-ot.org. Unnumbered lists remain <ul>/<li> which becomes DITA <ul><li> (or possibly <sl> for step-less lists if inside a task but not actual steps).
  • Tables are transformed to <table> with proper <tgroup><tbody><row> etc., aligning with DITA table model (which is similar to HTML).
  • Images are converted to DITA <image> elements (with href pointing to the media file and possibly <alt> text captured). We ensure figure captions from sources become either <title> inside an <fig> wrapper or other suitable markup.

  • Cross-references: If the source had cross-reference links, we need to convert those to DITA <xref> elements. The intermediate likely expresses links as HTML anchors or DocBook xlinks; our XSLT will detect references to other docs and convert them. (In DITA, cross-topic links use <xref href="otherTopic.dita">). We may need to adjust the href extensions if topics are split. All such link rewriting can be handled with data gathered during chunking (e.g., we know a source document “Guide123” became “Guide123_concept.dita” and “Guide123_task.dita”, etc., so we can update references accordingly).

  • Deterministic Transformation: The XSLT (or Python transformation code) is written to produce consistent output given the same input and config. There is no reliance on external state or random values. If we run this stage tomorrow on the same intermediate file, the DITA topic XML will be identical (barring timestamp metadata if any). We can enforce this by avoiding any nondeterministic functions and by sorting any ambiguous sequences (for example, if iterating over JSON keys, ensure a stable order). The principle "same inputs, same outputs" is strictly followed oai_citation:13‡medium.com, which not only aids debugging but also allows caching: we might skip this transform if we detect the intermediate file’s hash is unchanged from a previous run and the output DITA file already exists with matching hash.

  • Graceful Degradation in Transformation: If certain content cannot be perfectly mapped to DITA, we handle it gracefully. For example, DITA has no direct equivalent for some exotic constructs – say a complex table nested in a list might be tricky. Our XSLT can output a <fnote> or <draft-comment> with the original content as fallback, so the information isn’t lost. Additionally, if an entire section fails (e.g., XSLT throws an error on an unexpected element), we catch that (Saxon allows try-catch via XSLT 3.0 or we do it by segmenting the XSLT). The problematic section can be omitted or left as raw content in a <pre> inside a DITA <note> marked as needing attention. This way, the final DITA file is still well-formed and valid, and the pipeline continues, with the issue noted for later fix.

By the end of Stage 2, for each original input we have one or more DITA topic files (.dita XML) typed as concept, task, or reference. For example, a single Word file might have yielded three DITA topics: a concept intro, a task procedure, and a reference table – all derived according to rules. Each DITA topic will include the proper <title> (from the source’s title), optional <shortdesc>, and the content in the appropriate sections. These topics conform to either the base DITA DTD or a custom specialization DTD if specified (for instance, if using a specialized information type, the root element might be a custom <myConcept> which extends <concept> – Oxygen allows defining custom frameworks for such cases).

docs.title: Stage 3: Load – DITA Map Assembly and Output Deployment
docs.text:
Objective: Aggregate all the generated DITA topic files into a coherent collection, create DITA maps to organize them, and output the final artifacts to the designated location (file system, repository, etc.).

  • DITA Map Generation: A DITA map (*.ditamap) is essentially a TOC or structure file that references all topics. We automatically build a map to include the converted topics. The map could be flat or hierarchical, depending on input structure or configuration. For instance, if an input Word document had chapters and subchapters that we split into separate DITA files, the map will reflect that nesting with nested <topicref> elements. We use the info gathered during Stage 2 (the relationships between topics, original ordering, etc.) to order the <topicref> entries. The map’s title can be taken from the source project name or a config setting. We ensure each topicref has the correct linking attributes (e.g., linking to the topic’s file, and the @type attribute could be set to "concept/task/reference" accordingly for clarity). If the pipeline processes multiple top-level documents, we might create multiple maps or one master map that includes them as chapters (configurable). The output folder will contain the DITA map and all the .dita topic files (likely in subfolders if organized).

  • Applying Output Flavor Configurations: “Output flavors” in config could control variations in output. For example, you might have a config for different DITA editions (maybe one that produces Light DITA vs full DITA, or DITA 1.2 vs 1.3, or includes certain metadata for one type of output). Because our pipeline is config-driven, we could easily support multiple output flavors. For instance, if the config says output_flavor: "Lightweight DITA" we might produce MDITA (Markdown DITA) or HDITA (XHTML-based DITA) instead of full XML DITA. Or if output_flavor: "DITA 2.0" in future, we adjust the transformation accordingly. In this case, the user specifically requested plain DITA 1.3 (the standard XML-based DITA v1.3), so our pipeline ensures compliance with that version. If custom DTDs are to be used (e.g., a specialized concept type), those DTDs (or schemas) should be referenced in the Oxygen framework and the output documents should declare the proper DOCTYPE or XML namespace. We can allow the config to specify a doctype public identifier or schema location for the output files if needed, thereby supporting extended DITA frameworks.

  • Output to Specified Location: The pipeline writes all final artifacts to a target directory (which could be, for example, a folder ready to be imported into a CCMS or a Git repository). Each run could go to a timestamped folder or a versioned folder if needed (since artifacts are immutable, one could keep past runs separate). The names of output files are deterministic (e.g., based on input file names and maybe section titles), or a mapping can be provided in config (some systems want specific file naming conventions). Because we hashed inputs, we could also include a content hash in file names for uniqueness if required, but generally keeping original names is fine.

  • Validation and Post-Processing: After generation, we perform a validation pass. Oxygen’s scripting includes a “DITA Validate and Check for Completeness” operation oai_citation:14‡oxygenxml.com that can be run on the output map. We can invoke this to ensure all topic references are resolved, all required DITA elements are present, and the content meets DITA rules. Any validation error (e.g., a missing @href or a topic missing a title) would be reported. Ideally, our transformation logic avoids such errors, but this is a good safety net and aids testability. If validation finds issues, we log them. If critical (config can define what is critical), we might mark the pipeline run as failed. Otherwise, minor issues could be tolerated with warnings (graceful degradation, as long as output is mostly usable).

  • Integration with Downstream Tools: At this point, the DITA content is ready. If needed, we could call the DITA Open Toolkit to publish output (like HTML or PDF), but that’s beyond the scope of conversion. However, an automated pipeline might trigger such publishing as a next step. We might also package the DITA content (e.g., as a ZIP or deliver it to a CCMS). Because each topic and map is stored as a file, source control or further processing can treat them just like any DITA project. The pipeline can also output a conversion report (perhaps in JSON) summarizing: how many topics created, any errors or warnings, time taken for each stage, etc., which is useful for auditing and continuous improvement.

With Stage 3 complete, we have achieved the end goal: the source documents in dozens of formats have been transformed into a structured DITA 1.3 knowledge base, organized via maps. Writers or engineers can now use Oxygen XML Editor to open these DITA files for manual touch-ups if needed, or run DITA-OT to produce final deliverables. The pipeline’s output is deterministic and version-controlled, so re-running it on updated source docs will yield predictable updates in the DITA content.

docs.title: Configuration-Driven Processing
docs.text:
A cornerstone of this solution is the use of external configuration files (YAML/JSON) to drive behavior:

  • Mappings and Taxonomies: The config defines how source content maps to DITA structures. For example, a style-to-element map for Word: (“Heading 1” style -> Topic Title, “Code Block” style -> <code> in DITA, etc.), or for Markdown: (a YAML metadata field ditatype: task could indicate the whole file is a Task). It can also contain a taxonomy of content types or product categories to assign metadata in DITA (for instance, tagging topics with audience or category metadata based on file location).
  • Chunking Rules: As mentioned, config controls how to split content. E.g. chunk_by: "h1" or a numeric level. It could even allow more complex rules like “split before any H2 that has style ‘TopicStart’” or “every slide in a PowerPoint becomes a separate topic”. These rules are read by Stage 2 to actually split nodes.
  • Output Options: The config can list output preferences such as the DITA doctype (concept vs task vs reference or a custom one). It may allow defaulting everything to concept unless specified, or using certain keywords to auto-detect. It can also hold the path for output, naming conventions, etc. Additionally, if multiple outputs are needed (say, one set of DITA topics in English and another in a different language or a lightweight format), config can specify multiple output flavors to run sequentially or in parallel.

  • Example Config Snippet (pseudo-YAML): 

    source_formats:
  default_intermediate: "docbook5"
  treat_as_markdown: [".md", ".markdown", ".mdown"]
  treat_as_docx: [".pages"]  # e.g., we might rename .pages to .docx after conversion
classification_rules:
  by_filename:
    - pattern: "*_guide.*", topic_type: "concept"
    - pattern: "HOWTO_*", topic_type: "task"
  by_content:
    - pattern: "Step [0-9]+", topic_type: "task"
    - pattern: "Reference", topic_type: "reference"
chunking:
  level: 1  # split at first-level headings
  nested_topics: true  # allow nested <topic> in a single file for sub-sections
dita_output:
  dita_version: "1.3"
  use_specialization: false  # or name of specialized DTD
  output_folder: "out/dita"
  map_title: "Documentation Set"
    This illustrates how the pipeline might know what to do without hardcoding logic. The Python script reads these settings and the XSLT can also get parameters (Saxon allows passing parameters from command line, so we could pass chunkLevel=1 etc. to the stylesheet).

  • Version Control: The config files themselves are versioned (e.g. in Git). Any change to a mapping or rule is tracked. This ties into determinism and testing: if the output changed, we can pinpoint if it was due to a config change or a code change. Also, one can maintain different config versions for different projects or document sets.

Using a config-first approach makes the system flexible – if tomorrow you decide that a certain type of content should be classified differently, you update config rather than code. It also decouples what we want to achieve from how the code does it, aligning with the principle of contracts (the config is essentially a contract for behavior).

docs.title: Automation and Orchestration
docs.text:
A Python-based orchestration will coordinate these stages, ensuring the pipeline runs smoothly end-to-end:

  • Python Orchestration Script: A single Python script (or package) will implement the pipeline as a sequence of functions or classes corresponding to stages. For instance, extract_stage(input_path) -> intermediate_path, transform_stage(intermediate_path) -> [topic_files], load_stage(topic_files) -> ditamap_file. We keep these functions small and focused (isolate concerns). The main script will iterate over input files (supporting batch mode by reading a folder or a manifest file). Python’s rich standard library and third-party modules help here: e.g., use subprocess to call Pandoc or Oxygen CLI tools; use lxml or xml.etree if any inline XML tweaks needed in Python; use PyYAML or json to parse config files. We also use logging to track progress and issues.

  • Integration with Oxygen and Saxon: To run XSLT (for DocBook-to-DITA or similar), we have a few choices. One is to call Oxygen’s command-line Transform script, which can apply a predefined transformation scenario. Another is to call Saxon directly. Saxon HE (open-source) can be invoked by executing a .jar – e.g., java -jar saxon-he.jar -s:input.xml -xsl:transform.xsl -o:output.xml param1=.... We can embed such a call in Python. Since the user has Oxygen, they might also use the Oxygen SDK to integrate more tightly, but that typically involves Java programming. In Python, calling the external process is simpler. Oxygen’s Batch Converter script (as noted in Stage 1) is similarly called via shell (e.g., sh batchConverter.sh -i my.docx -if docx -o out -of dita). Our script can dynamically decide: “if format is docx and we trust Oxygen’s converter, call that directly to get DITA.” Or use Pandoc first, etc.

  • Parallel Processing: If dealing with a large number of files, we could parallelize the extraction stage easily (since each file is independent). Python’s concurrency (multiprocessing or multithreading for I/O bound tasks) can speed up conversion, especially when calling external tools. Care must be taken to not overwhelm CPU or memory (e.g., limit to a few parallel Pandoc instances). The transform stage could also potentially run in parallel for independent files, but if it’s XSLT via Saxon, it might be simpler to do sequentially unless using multiple Saxon instances. The orchestration could be managed by a job queue or just a loop with asynchronous calls. Regardless, parallelism should not compromise determinism – order of outputs doesn’t matter if each is complete, but for logging consistency and final map ordering, we might collect results and sort them by name.

  • Optional Workflow Frameworks: For more complex pipelines or integration into a larger system, one could use a workflow tool like Apache Airflow, Luigi, or Prefect to define the ETL steps and handle scheduling, retries, etc. For example, Luigi (a Python framework) could have tasks like ConvertToDocBookTask, TransformToDITATask, etc., with dependencies. This adds robustness (automatic rerun on failure, easier monitoring). However, if the pipeline is straightforward, a custom Python script or Makefile might suffice. Another lightweight option is using DVC (Data Version Control) which can manage pipeline stages with outputs tracked by hashes, aligning well with our approach of hashing each step’s outputs (DVC can skip stages if input/output unchanged). The user can choose the complexity based on need – since the question suggests a script, the answer focuses on that, but indeed these frameworks could be introduced as the pipeline scales.

  • Integration Testing the Pipeline: The orchestrator can include a self-test mode where it runs through known sample files.
